coredata.dc:title: Development of a fluorescent nano-sensing probe for atomoxetine analysis: Additional clinical pharmacokinetic study
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Alosaimi, Manal E.
Abduljabbar, Maram H.
Alzhrani, Rami M.
Saeed, Salma
Ramzy, Sherif
Almalki, Atiah H.
coredata.dc:description: Atomoxetine is a psychostimulant drug used for the treatment of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) symptoms in people with autism. Herein, eco-friendly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using black-eyed pea beans and characterized for the purpose of quantifying atomoxetine in pharmaceutical capsules and human plasma. The selectivity of these CQDs towards atomoxetine was improved by functionalizing their surface with an atomoxetine-tetraphenylborate ion complex. The quantification of atomoxetine is based on measuring the fluorescence quenching of the functionalized CQDs in response to varying concentrations of atomoxetine. The Stern-Volmer plot was employed to investigate the mechanism through which atomoxetine quenches the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs. The outcomes indicated a dynamic quenching mechanism. The applied method was optimized and validated in compliance with ICH requirements, resulting in excellent linearity across the concentration range of 50–800 ng/mL. The developed method was successfully used to quantify atomoxetine in pharmaceutical dosage form and human plasma with acceptable accuracy and precision outcomes. In addition, the method was applied for clinical pharmacokinetic study of atomoxetine in the plasma of children diagnosed with both autism and ADHD. Atomoxetine was rapidly absorbed after a single oral dose of 10 mg, reaching maximum concentration within two hours and having a half-life (t1/2) of 3.11 h. Moreover, the method demonstrates a notable degree of eco-friendliness, as evidenced by two greenness evaluation metrics; Green Analytical Procedure Index (GAPI) and Analytical GREEnness (AGREE).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Atomoxetine
ADHD
Autism
Fluorescent nano-sensing probe
Clinical pharmacokinetic study

Analysis of AMT in Atomox Apex® capsules
Bioanalytical procedure in spiked human plasma
Validation of the developed method according to ICH Q2(R1) guidelines
Limit of detection limit (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CQDs; (b) FTIR spectrum of CQDs; (c) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs; (d) Excitation and emission spectra of CQDs; (e) excitation dependent emission spectra of CQDs.
(a) Fluorescence quenching spectra of CQDs by different AMT concentrations; (b) Stern-Volmer plot at different temperature.
Mean plasma concentration, normalized to a 1 mg/Kg dose, against time curve for AMT after single dose.
Greenness examination of the developed method.
Selectivity studies in the presence of various interferent substances.
Analysis of AMT in Atomox Apex® capsules by the proposed method and statistical comparison with the reported method [16].

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values in parentheses are tabulated values of t and F at P = 0.05.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis of AMT in spiked plasma.
Results of the clinical pharmacokinetic study of AMT.

head.ce:title: Development of a fluorescent nano-sensing probe for atomoxetine analysis: Additional clinical pharmacokinetic study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Atomoxetine is a psychostimulant drug used for the treatment of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) symptoms in people with autism. Herein, eco-friendly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using black-eyed pea beans and characterized for the purpose of quantifying atomoxetine in pharmaceutical capsules and human plasma. The selectivity of these CQDs towards atomoxetine was improved by functionalizing their surface with an atomoxetine-tetraphenylborate ion complex. The quantification of atomoxetine is based on measuring the fluorescence quenching of the functionalized CQDs in response to varying concentrations of atomoxetine. The Stern-Volmer plot was employed to investigate the mechanism through which atomoxetine quenches the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs. The outcomes indicated a dynamic quenching mechanism. The applied method was optimized and validated in compliance with ICH requirements, resulting in excellent linearity across the concentration range of 50–800 ng/mL. The developed method was successfully used to quantify atomoxetine in pharmaceutical dosage form and human plasma with acceptable accuracy and precision outcomes. In addition, the method was applied for clinical pharmacokinetic study of atomoxetine in the plasma of children diagnosed with both autism and ADHD. Atomoxetine was rapidly absorbed after a single oral dose of 10 mg, reaching maximum concentration within two hours and having a half-life (t1/2) of 3.11 h. Moreover, the method demonstrates a notable degree of eco-friendliness, as evidenced by two greenness evaluation metrics; Green Analytical Procedure Index (GAPI) and Analytical GREEnness (AGREE).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Autism, a complex neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by challenges in social interaction and communication, and ADHD, a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by symptoms of inattention, impulsivity, and hyperactivity, are both recognized as significant neuropsychiatric conditions that often co-occur with a notable prevalence of comorbidity between them [1]. Children who have both autism and ADHD tend to exhibit a greater number of autism symptoms in the long run [2]. Research has indicated that individuals with a dual diagnosis of autism and ADHD are more likely to experience cognitive delays at a higher rate than those with either autism or ADHD alone [3].

Atomoxetine hydrochloride (AMT), Fig. 1 , presents an interesting psychostimulant medication for the management of symptoms associated with ADHD in individuals diagnosed with autism. This drug functions as a noradrenergic reuptake inhibitor, resulting in elevated levels of norepinephrine [4]. Due to the potential serious side effects commonly linked to the administration of neuropsychiatric medication, it is important to establish an analytical approach that is appropriate for the therapeutic monitoring of these drugs. This method aims to determine the precise dosage for each individual, ensuring maximal efficacy while minimizing adverse reactions [5]. Therefore, numerous analytical techniques have been established to facilitate the quantitative assessment of AMT in both pharmaceutical and biological matrices. These techniques include gas chromatography [6], liquid chromatography [7–11], electrochemical analysis [12,13], spectrofluorimetry [14–16], and spectrophotometry [16–18]. These techniques, despite their potential for therapeutic monitoring of AMT, have significant limitations, including high costs, lack of environmental friendliness, and the need for pretreatment and derivatization procedures. Consequently, it is essential to create new approaches that are uncomplicated, economic, and environmentally sustainable.

Recently, there has been a significant focus on the utilization of CQDs sourced from organic herbs or waste biomass for the purpose of serving as a fluorescent nano-sensing probe in the quantification of pharmaceutical compounds. In addition to exhibiting the typical characteristics of traditional CQDs, such as a large surface area, electroactivity, conductivity, physicochemical stability, and excellent optical properties, they are widely regarded as eco- friendly.

The key objective of this study was to fabricate eco-friendly fluorescent CQDs from black-eyed pea beans for the purpose of quantifying AMT in pharmaceutical capsules and human plasma. To improve the selectivity of the produced CQDs towards AMT, their surfaces were functionalized with an AMT-tetraphenylborate ion association complex. The quantification was based on measuring the fluorescence quenching of functionalized CQDs in response to various AMT concentrations. Following optimization and validation studies, the developed method proved to be reliable and suitable for monitoring the therapeutic levels of AMT. Consequently, the method was applied for clinical pharmacokinetic study of atomoxetine in the plasma of children diagnosed with both autism and ADHD after single oral dose. Furthermore, the degree of environmental sustainability of the applied method was evaluated through the utilization of GAPI [19] and AGREE [20] metrics, leading to findings that confirm the ecological sustainability of the method.

The standard powder of AMT (Purity 99.33 %) was provided by Multi-Apex Pharma (Egypt). Atomox Apex® capsules containing 10 mg of AMT per capsule were produced by Multi-Apex Pharma (Egypt) and obtained from a pharmacy. All reagents and solvents utilized in the experiment were of analytical grade and were sourced from local vendors. The water employed in the process was freshly double distilled. The black-eyed peas were acquired from the market in Egypt.

A standard stock solution (100 µg/mL) of AMT was prepared through dissolving 10 mg of AMT standard powder in 60 mL of distilled water within a 100-mL volumetric flask. The solution was agitated until complete dissolution was achieved, followed by the addition of distilled water to reach the flask's calibration mark. Subsequently, a working solution (10 μg/mL) was derived from the aforementioned standard stock solution through additional dilution with distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CQDs from black-eyed pea
ce:section.ce:para: The black-eyed pea beans underwent washing with distilled water and subsequent drying at ambient conditions prior to being pulverized into a fine powder. An exact amount of the powder, equivalent to 1 gm, was placed in a porcelain dish and subjected to heating in an electric oven adjusted to a temperature of 250 ℃ for a period of 30 min. The carbonized powder was dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water following its cooling to room temperature. The resulting solution was then heated to 80 ℃ while being stirred on a hot plate for a duration of 10 min. Subsequent to cooling, the solution underwent filtration and was then subjected to centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 20 min. The resulting yellow supernatant solution was subsequently dried using a rotary evaporator, leaving behind a powder residue containing the CQDs, which was allowed to dry further at room temperature.

The Shimadzu UV–Visible 1800 Spectrophotometer (Japan) and Jasco FP-6200 spectrofluorometer (Japan) were employed for the determination of absorbance and fluorescence optical characteristics of the CQDs. The examination of surface morphological properties and structural functional groups of the CQDs were conducted utilizing a JEOL JEM-M2100 transmission electron microscope (USA) and a PerkinElmer Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (USA).

The formation of the ion association complex of AMT-tetraphenylborate was initiated through the addition of 50 mL of sodium tetraphenylborate solution (10−2 M) into a conical flask that already contained 50 mL of AMT solution (10−2 M). Following this crucial step, the mixture was left to interact with its mother liquor for a duration of 4 h, allowing for the precipitation process to reach completion. The precipitate was then carefully filtered and thoroughly rinsed with distilled water. Finally, the filtered precipitate was left to air dry at ambient temperature for a period of 24 h, ensuring complete evaporation of moisture.

A total of 100 mg of CQDs powder was mixed with 300 mg of AMT-tetraphenylborate complex powder within a conical flask. Following this initial step, the combination of powders was dissolved in 70 mL of distilled water and sonicated for 1.5 h. Subsequent to the sonication procedure, the resulting solution was subjected to filtration and then underwent dialysis for 8 h using distilled water. After dialysis, the solution was carefully transferred to a 100-mL volumetric flask, adjusted to its final volume with water, and stored at 4 °C.

The calibration curve was established using seven AMT calibration solutions with final concentrations ranging from 50 to 800 ng/mL. These calibration solutions were made by combining 1 mL of functionalized CQDs solution with aliquots from the working AMT solution, ranging from 500-8000 ng AMT, in a sequence of 10-mL volumetric flasks. The solutions were adequately left to incubate at room temperature for 3 min, following which their total volume was adjusted by the addition of distilled water. Subsequently, the emission signals (F) of these solutions were recorded at 491 nm after excitation at 400 nm. Furthermore, a blank solution was produced under identical circumstances, and its emission signal (F0) was recorded at the same wavelengths. The F0/F ratio values were computed and graphed against the corresponding AMT concentrations to generate the calibration plot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of AMT in Atomox Apex® capsules
ce:section.ce:para: The contents of ten capsules of Atomox Apex® (each capsule labeled to contain 10 mg of AMT) were combined and weighed. The weight of the powder equal to that of a single capsule was dissolved in 60 mL of distilled water. Following vigorous agitation for 30 min, the solution was filtered into a 100-mL volumetric flask, and subsequently, the total volume was adjusted with distilled water. Five distinct samples with varying concentrations of AMT were formulated and analyzed in accordance with the outlined procedure for constructing a calibration curve. The AMT concentrations present in the prepared samples were determined by utilizing the regression equation obtained from the calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioanalytical procedure in spiked human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: To analyze AMT in human plasma, a series of five calibration standards (80, 150, 300, 500, and 800 ng/mL) along with quality control (QC) samples (80, 200, 300, and 700 ng/mL) were prepared by adding precise amounts of AMT working stock solution to 1 mL human plasma. Each sample underwent protein precipitation with the addition of 3 mL of acetonitrile, followed by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 30 min. The resulting supernatants were subjected to evaporation, and the residues were reconstituted with 2 mL of water before being transferred into 10-mL volumetric flasks. The steps outlined in the calibration curve procedure were carefully followed.

Five autistic children aged 7 to 12 years who had recently been diagnosed with ADHD symptoms were admitted to Al-Azhar University Hospital in New Damietta, Egypt, before to beginning AMT treatment. Blood samples were obtained immediately before administering Atomox Apex® 10 mg capsule, as well as 1, 2, 4, 8, and 12 h after the single dose. The samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 25 min to separate the plasma. Plasma samples were kept frozen at − 20 °C until analysis. The plasma samples were subsequently analyzed using the bioanalytical procedure described above, and the concentrations of AMT were calculated. The concentration–time curve was created, and the pharmacokinetic data were obtained using Microsoft Excel's PK Solver.

The characterization of the fabricated CQDs involved the utilization of various analytical techniques such as transmission electron microscope (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer, UV–Vis spectrophotometer, and spectrofluorometer. The surface morphology analysis of the CQDs was specifically conducted through TEM. This examination was performed under the conditions of a 200 KV operating voltage to ensure detailed visualization of the nanostructures. The TEM image, as displayed in Fig. 2 a, provided a clear depiction of the CQDs' uniformity, demonstrating spherical shapes with an average diameter of 3 nm. The utilization of FTIR spectrometry was implemented in order to examine and analyze the various functional groups that are present on the surface of the CQDs. This analytical technique, as demonstrated in the FTIR spectrum illustrated in Fig. 2b, revealed distinct signals observed at specific wavenumbers of 3386 cm−1, 3155 cm−1, 2932 cm−1, 1716 cm−1, 1621 cm−1, 1368 cm−1, 1260 cm−1, and 1021 cm−1. These signals were found to be associated with the presence and characteristics of different functional groups, including N − H, O − H, C–H, C = O, C = C, COO–, C − N, and C − O groups, respectively.

The optical properties of the CQDs were investigated through the utilization of a UV–Vis spectrophotometer and a spectrofluorometer. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CQDs exhibited a predominant peak at 256 nm, accompanied by a tail that extended towards the visible spectrum. This observation agrees with the electronic transition from π to π* of the C = C moiety and from n to π* of the C = O group, as illustrated in Fig. 2c. Moreover, following excitation at a wavelength of 400 nm, it was observed that the CQDs exhibited a significant peak in fluorescence emission at 491 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 2d. Furthermore, an in-depth analysis of the excitation-dependent properties of CQDs was conducted by varying the excitation wavelength range between 400 and 470 nm. Consequently, a noticeable shift towards longer wavelengths was detected in the emission fluorescence peak, transitioning from 491 to 525 nm, in conjunction with a marked decrease in fluorescence intensity, upon the adjustment of excitation to higher wavelengths. This observation serves to validate the excitation-dependent emission characteristics inherent in CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2e.

The quantum yield of CQDs was determined by comparing with quinine sulfate as a fluorescence reference standard in a solution of 0.01 M sulfuric acid at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm, as outlined in the subsequent equation [21]:
(1)
φ
s =
φ r
A r
A s
E s
E r
η
s
2
η
r
2
The symbol Φ represents the fluorescence quantum yield, while η denotes the refractive index of the solvent; A stands for the absorbance of the solution, and E represents the integrated fluorescence intensity (area) of the emitted light. The subscripts “r” and “s” are used to distinguish between the reference and sample, respectively. The quantum yield of CQDs derived from black-eyed pea beans was determined to be 32 %.

The selectivity of the synthesized CQDs towards the detection of AMT was enhanced through the activation of their surface using an AMT-tetraphenylborate complex. Interaction between the functionalized CQDs and AMT results in a decline in the fluorescence intensity of the functionalized CQDs. The level of AMT present is a critical factor in the process of fluorescence quenching. As AMT concentration increases, more AMT molecules bind with functionalized CQDs, resulting in increasing fluorescence quenching. This relationship between concentration and fluorescence quenching can be accurately quantified, as illustrated in Fig. 3 a, where the fluorescence intensity of the functionalized CQDs diminishes progressively with increasing AMT concentration.

There are three possible mechanisms for AMT-induced fluorescence quenching of CQDs: the inner filter effect, dynamic quenching, and static quenching. Since AMT does not exhibit UV absorption responses within the 300–600 nm wavelength range, there is no inner filter effect from AMT at the excitation and emission wavelengths of CQDs, suggesting that the quenching is not associated with the inner filter effect. A study was carried out to investigate the temperature dependency in order to ascertain whether the quenching process is of a dynamic or static nature through the application of the Stern-Volmer equation:
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
⌈ Q ⌉
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CQDs in the absence and presence of quencher, KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant (slope), and Q is the quencher concentration.

At three distinct temperatures (25 °C, 30 °C, and 40 °C), three calibration curves were constructed by graphing the correlation between F0/F and AMT concentrations, and subsequently, the Stern-Volmer equations were formulated based on these curves. In dynamic quenching, the Stern-Volmer quenching constant increases with temperature, but in static quenching, it decreases with temperature. As shown in Fig. 3b, the Stern-Volmer quenching constant value increased with increasing temperature, indicating that the quenching mechanism resulting from the interaction of AMT and CQDs is dynamic.

To assess the impact of the pH on fluorescence quenching, a study was conducted, incorporating the use of buffer solutions of varying pH values and comparing results with and without their addition. It was observed that altering the pH values did not yield a noteworthy effect on the results. Consequently, the necessity for a buffer solution throughout the procedure was deemed unnecessary, thereby improving the environmental sustainability of the approach.

A study was carried out to investigate the impact of different volumes of the CQDs solution on fluorescence quenching. It was observed from the results that the utilization of 1 mL of the CQDs solution resulted in the most efficient fluorescence quenching when binding with AMT.

The investigation was conducted to determine the most suitable incubation period for achieving complete binding of AMT to the surface of CQDs, with a focus on monitoring fluorescence quenching at various time points. The findings demonstrated that the most effective fluorescence quenching of CQDs occurred within 3 min, suggesting a rapid interaction between the CQDs and AMT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the developed method according to ICH Q2(R1) guidelines

ce:section.ce:para: The determination of linearity and working range in this study was accomplished through the plotting of the ratio of F0/F against the concentrations of AMT in ng/mL. The graphical representation of the regression plot exhibited a linear relationship within the working range from 50 to 800 ng/mL. Table 1 contains detailed information about the linearity and range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limit of detection limit (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ)
ce:section.ce:para: LOD and LOQ were determined utilizing mathematical equations, specifically derived from the expressions: 3.3 times the standard deviation divided by the slope for LOD, and 10 times the standard deviation divided by the slope for LOQ. The LOD and LOQ values of the applied method were 14.32 ng/mL and 43.40 ng/mL, indicating good sensitivity (Table 1).

To investigate the accuracy and precision of the developed method, three samples containing final concentrations of 100 ng/mL, 300 ng/mL, and 600 ng/mL of AMT were prepared in accordance with the procedure outlined in the calibration curve. In order to assess accuracy, each of these samples underwent analysis three times, and subsequently, the mean percentage recovery (%R) values were computed. On the other hand, to investigate repeatability and intermediate precision, each sample was subjected to analysis three times within the same day and across three consecutive days, respectively. Following this, the relative standard deviation (RSD) values were determined.
The obtained findings revealed high accuracy and precision, with %R of 99.91 % and RSD value of 0.81 for repeatability precision and of 1.05 for intermediate precision, as shown in Table 1.

The examination of the selectivity of the nano-sensing probe synthesized for the detection of AMT involved the introduction of various interferants including K+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Starch, dimethicone, glutamate, albumin, risperidone, aripiprazole, and venlafaxine. The results in Table 2 show that the calculated quenching percentage for all interfering species was within a margin of 5 %, demonstrating that the approach is highly selective for AMT.

Five children diagnosed with both autism and ADHD, ranging in age from 7 to 12 years (mean of 8.8 ± 2.2), were selected to participate in this study. All participants were identified as CYP2D6 extensive metabolizers. A single Atomox Apex® 10 mg capsule was administered to all children, with dosages varying from 0.24 to 0.48 mg/kg of body weight. The plasma concentrations depicted in Fig. 4 were standardized to a dose of 1 mg/kg for the purpose of facilitating graphical comparison. Following oral administration, AMT was quickly absorbed and reached maximal concentration within two hours. The mean maximum plasma concentrations (Cmax) and half-life (t1/2) observed after dose standardization were 575 ng/mL and 3.11 h. The results of the pharmacokinetic study of AMT are summarized in Table 5 .

The compliance of the newly established approach with the fundamental principles of green analytical chemistry was assessed using two distinct greenness evaluation metrics: GAPI and AGREE. As shown in Fig. 5 , the approach achieved an AGREE score of 0.81, and most of the GAPI pictograms were shaded green, indicating that the approach adheres closely to the fundamental principles of green analytical chemistry.

A precise, selective, and eco-friendly spectrofluorometric approach was established utilizing CQDs sourced from black-eyed pea beans for the precise and selective quantification of AMT through dynamic fluorescence quenching pattern. The selectivity of these CQDs for AMT was improved by functionalizing their surface with an AMT-tetraphenylborate complex. The method was highly sensitive allowing for quantification of AMT in both pharmaceutical capsules and human plasma. The method was successfully applied for the clinical pharmacokinetic study of AMT in the plasma of children diagnosed with autism and ADHA after a single oral dose. Moreover, the approach demonstrates a significant level of environmental sustainability which can be linked to the derivation of the CQDs from a naturally biocompatible source, the employment of water as the main solvent, the lack of any pre-treatment or alteration processes, and the utilization of energy-efficient equipment.
Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2024R466), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

Manal E. Alosaimi: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Maram H. Abduljabbar: Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation. Rami M. Alzhrani: Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Salma Saeed: Validation, Methodology. Sherif Ramzy: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Atiah H. Almalki: Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2024R466), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inattention and hyperactivity in association with autism spectrum disorders in the CHARGE study
Res. Autism Spectr. Disorders

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Lyall, J.B. Schweitzer, R.J. Schmidt, I. Hertz-Picciotto, M. Solomon, Inattention and hyperactivity in association with autism spectrum disorders in the CHARGE study,Research in autism spectrum disorders35 (2017) 1-12. DOI: 10.1016/j.rasd.2016.11.011

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The co-occurrence of autism and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder in children–what do we know?
Front. Hum. Neurosci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Leitner, The co-occurrence of autism and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder in children–what do we know?.Front. Hum. Neurosci. 8 (2014) 268. DOI: 10.3389/fnhum.2014.00268

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Association between severity of behavioral phenotype and comorbid attention deficit hyperactivity disorder symptoms in children with autism spectrum disorders
Autism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.A. Rao, R.J. Landa, Association between severity of behavioral phenotype and comorbid attention deficit hyperactivity disorder symptoms in children with autism spectrum disorders,Autism18 (2014) 272-280. DOI: 10.1177/1362361312470494

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Difference in serotonergic and noradrenergic regulation of human social behaviours
Psychopharmacol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.S. Tse, A.J. Bond, Difference in serotonergic and noradrenergic regulation of human social behaviours,Psychopharmacol.159 (2002) 216-221. DOI: 10.1007/s00213-001-0926-9

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Therapeutic drug monitoring of atomoxetine in children and adolescents with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder: a naturalistic study
J. Neural Transm.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Ruppert, C. Geffert, H.W. Clement, C. Bachmann, M. Haberhausen, E. Schulz, C. Fleischhaker, M. Biscaldi-Schäfer, Therapeutic drug monitoring of atomoxetine in children and adolescents with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder: a naturalistic study, J. Neural Transm. 129 (2022) 945-959. DOI: 10.1007/s00702-022-02483-8

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel GC–MS assay method for the therapeutic drug monitoring of the atomoxetine
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kul, M. Ozdemir, A. Önal, O. Sagirli, A novel GC–MS assay method for the therapeutic drug monitoring of the atomoxetine, Microchem. J. 164 (2021) 105953. DOI: 10.1016/j.microc.2021.105953
